message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-9 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use "python -m pytest"/"source .venv/bin/activate"; you may run gh for CI claims.

FINDING P9-PII-1 (medium, PRIV): Operator's internal hostname ([REDACTED] / [REDACTED]) leaks into committed transcripts; no scrub pattern covers it
Location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-216 (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _scrub: no hostname rule); leaked in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl and wf_496c86b1-0fc/agent-a6c39cf0b12e80f58.jsonl and wf_574c42b7-0aa/agent-ad2e7e5365e7451dd.jsonl
Claim: The capture scrubber redacts email, home-dir username, display name, and GitHub handle, but has NO rule for the machine hostname. The operator's internal hostname appears in the committed corpus as the strings '[REDACTED]' and '[REDACTED]' (14 raw occurrences across 2+ files). At least one is genuine data, not meta-discussion: a `user`-role message embeds the PROV-O RDF namespace `hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file. `hostname`/`scutil --get LocalHostName`/`--get ComputerName` on this node all return [REDACTED], and per the operator memory this Mac mini is the named production node, so the hostname is operator/infrastructure-identifying PII.
Why real: Verified live: `scutil --get LocalHostName` and `ComputerName` both return `[REDACTED]`; `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]`. Importing the scrubber and calling capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node') returns the string UNCHANGED — confirming no pattern matches the hostname. The string is present in committed files (grep -rl finds it), so it ships in the repo and git history. This is a distinct identity class from everything pass 7 covered (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate this finding by examining the actual code and verifying the claims.

content.content:
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
117	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
118	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
119	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
120	        toks = n.split()
121	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
122	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
123	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
124	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
125	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
126	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
127	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
128	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
129	        for t in toks:
130	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
131	                seen.add(t.lower())
132	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
133	    return pats
134	
135	
136	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
137	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
138	
139	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
140	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
141	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
142	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
143	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
144	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
145	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
146	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
147	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
148	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
149	    """
150	    handles: list[str] = []
151	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
152	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
153	    try:
154	        import subprocess
155	
156	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
157	        if out.returncode == 0:
158	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
159	                handles.append(m.group(1))
160	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
161	        pass
162	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
163	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    expanded: list[str] = []
165	    for h in handles:
166	        expanded.append(h)
167	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
168	            expanded.append(h[3:])
169	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
170	    seen: set[str] = set()
171	    for h in expanded:
172	        key = h.lower()
173	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
174	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
175	        seen.add(key)
176	        if len(h) >= 8:
177	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
178	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
179	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
180	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
181	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
182	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
183	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
184	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
185	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
186	        else:
187	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	    return pats
189	
190	
191	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
192	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
193	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
194	
195	
196	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
197	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
198	        if pat.groups == 2:
199	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
200	        elif pat.groups == 1:
201	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
202	        else:
203	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
204	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
205	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
206	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
207	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
208	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
209	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
210	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
211	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
212	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
213	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
214	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
215	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
216	    return text
217	
218	
219	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
220	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
221	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
222	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
223	
224	
225	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
226	    try:
227	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
228	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
229	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json

content.text:
The finding holds up on every checkable claim:

1. **Hostname confirmed live**: `scutil --get LocalHostName` = `[REDACTED]`, `hostname` = `[REDACTED]`. Per operator memory this Mac mini is the named production node.

2. **Scrubber has no hostname rule and does not catch it**: `capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node')` and the PROV-O namespace string both return UNCHANGED. The `_SECRET_PATTERNS` (lines 38-56) plus the derived username/display-name/handle rules cover email, home-dir username, project-dir slug, display name, and GitHub handle — none match a `.local` hostname.

3. **It ships in the committed corpus**: 22 raw occurrences across the 3 cited files, all git-tracked.

4. **At least one is genuine data, not meta-discussion**: the user-role message embeds `PREFIX hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file (with an internal IP `192.168.1.50` nearby) — not a discussion of the hostname.

5. **Distinct identity class** from pass 7's coverage (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).

This is a real PROV/PII leak. The infrastructure-identifying internal hostname of the named production node ships in committed transcripts, and the scrubber that was explicitly designed to mask operator identity (#F-024, #G-007, #J-002, #J-008) silently misses it. There is no documented scope caveat in README or GOVERNANCE.md exempting hostnames — the only scrub caveat referenced in code comments concerns reasoning content and path structure, not host identity. The "transcripts are intentionally committed as provenance" mandate is exactly why the scrubber exists; an uncovered identity class is a gap in that mitigation, not an in-scope design choice.

The finding stands.

input.reasoning:
Every checkable claim in P9-PII-1 verified against live system and committed code/data:

1) Hostname is real and operator/infra-identifying: `scutil --get LocalHostName` and `ComputerName` return `[REDACTED]`; `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]`. Operator memory designates this Mac mini as the named production node.

2) No scrub rule covers it, and it is NOT caught: `_SECRET_PATTERNS` (capture_transcripts.py:38-56) plus the derived `_username_pattern`/`_display_name_patterns`/`_repo_handle_patterns` cover email, home-dir username, project-dir slug, display name, and GitHub handle only. Running `capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node')` returns the string UNCHANGED; same for the PROV-O namespace `hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#>`.

3) It ships in the repo: 22 raw `[REDACTED]`/`[REDACTED]` occurrences across the 3 cited files, all confirmed git-tracked (git ls-files --error-unmatch succeeds).

4) At least one occurrence is genuine data, not meta-discussion: a user-role message embeds `PREFIX hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file (an internal IP 192.168.1.50 appears nearby), confirming the finding's specific example.

5) Distinct identity class from prior passes (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).

Refutation paths fail: no README/GOVERNANCE caveat exempts hostnames (grep found none); the only documented scrub caveat concerns reasoning content and path structure. The scrubber exists precisely to mask operator identity in intentionally-committed provenance transcripts (#F-024/#G-007/#J-002/#J-008), so an uncovered identity class is a gap in that mitigation, not an in-scope design choice. This is a real, unmitigated PII/provenance leak. Severity medium is consistent with the prior accepted identity-leak findings.

content.text: The finding STANDS. The operator's internal hostname leaks into committed transcripts as genuine data, and the identity-scrubber that exists specifically to mask operator PII in the intentionally-committed provenance corpus has no rule covering it. Relevant file: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-216`.
